content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Contextual Prompting for Robustness Against Adversarial Jailbreaks in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to jailbreaks and adversarial prompts that circumvent safety filters and generate harmful outputs. Existing static prompting and instruction tuning approaches struggle to detect and defend against sophisticated, context-adaptive attacks.",
  "motivation": "LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive real-world applications, making their resilience to adversarial manipulation critical. Growing evidence shows that LLMs can be tricked by prompt injections, paraphrased attacks, or contextually ambiguous cues. New prompting strategies are urgently needed to dynamically counter evolving attack vectors.",
  "method": "We propose a dynamic contextual prompting (DCP) method that interleaves the user's query with randomized, semantically-related 'safety anchor' prompts and decoy instructions. These anchors are drawn from a curated pool of safe task descriptions and benign outputs, inserted at strategic points in the conversational context. The LLM is thus primed to maintain alignment with safe behavior, as each generation step is conditioned not only on the adversarial input but also on additional safe exemplars, making it harder for adversaries to shape output distribution via prompt engineering. Automated ablations will optimize anchor diversity and insertion frequency.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate DCP on jailbreak/adversarial prompt datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) using GPT-3.5/4 and Llama-2 as testbeds. Baselines will include vanilla prompting and existing safety-tuned prompts. Metrics will be attack success rate, harmlessness score, and utility preservation on benign user queries."
}
